moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Hungarian Opening, an offbeat choice that prioritizes fianchettoing our kingside bishop on g2. This move is deceptively flexible—we're not committing to a central pawn yet, allowing us to adapt to Black's setup while controlling the long diagonal. The main idea is to build a solid, hypermodern position where we control the center with pieces rather than pawns, and g3 prepares Bg2, which exerts tremendous pressure along the a1-h8 diagonal.

Black immediately stakes a claim in the center with a central pawn break, signaling intention to control d5 and fight for space. This is the most natural response to our restrained setup—Black doesn't want to allow us a comfortable position. Notice Black hasn't committed fully to any particular system yet; d5 is flexible enough to support various plans depending on how we respond.

Our bishop lands on the long diagonal, aiming straight at b7 and eyeing central squares like d5 and e3. This is the core of our setup—the fianchettoed bishop is our most important piece, controlling key squares and supporting our eventual central operations. With Bg2 in place, we've established the hypermodern framework and can now build our position methodically, whether playing c4 later or supporting central pressure with moves like Nf3 and e4.

Black plays the Slav setup, preparing to support the d5 pawn with a rock-solid foundation and keeping options open for d5-d4 or dxc4 later. The move also prepares b5 in some lines and ensures the c-file remains defensible. This makes Black's position very stable and flexible—Black isn't overcommitting, but rather building a robust pawn skeleton that can withstand our hypermodern pressure.
